output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.5, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.95, "R1.4": 0.3, "R1.5": 0.2, "R1.6": 0.3, "R1.7": 0.9}}

Summary: A critique focused on misrepresented prior work, a null-result misinterpretation, and a stimulus design that is mismatched to the claim being made.

**The two cited papers appear to be inverted.** Krishnapriyan et al. (2021), "Characterizing possible failure modes in physics-informed neural networks," argues the *opposite* of what is summarized here: they show that vanilla PINN training fails on convection-dominated and multi-scale regimes, and they motivate sequence-to-sequence / curriculum training as a remedy. Citing them to justify *not* using curriculum training is a direct reversal of their thesis. Similarly, the Wang et al. line of work (gradient pathologies, NTK-based and learning-rate-annealing weighting) shows adaptive weighting *mitigates* — not "eliminates" — gradient imbalance, and the claim that it "previously prevented PINN convergence on stiff problems" is too strong. Please re-read both papers and rewrite this paragraph; as written, a reviewer who knows the literature will reject on this alone.

**A non-significant discrimination result is not evidence of physical faithfulness.** The 95% CI [49.6%, 56.6%] is compatible with no signal, but it is also compatible with a true discrimination rate of 56% — i.e., real but subtle artifacts that some experts catch. The Discussion treats failure-to-reject as confirmation of the null, which is the standard misuse. At minimum: (a) pre-register or compute the effect size you can rule out, (b) report a TOST or equivalence test against a pre-specified margin, and (c) replace "we conclude that PINN solutions are physically faithful" with a bounded claim about visual indistinguishability under this rendering.

**The stimulus is a near-worst-case discriminator for PINN failure modes.** Velocity-magnitude contours, unlabeled, with identical color limits, hide exactly the things PINNs get wrong: divergence-free violations, vorticity-sheet smearing, boundary-layer thinning near the moving lid, pressure-gradient artifacts, and absolute-scale errors (which the shared colormap removes by construction). A faithful test would include vorticity, divergence, streamlines through corner eddies, residual fields, and centerline u/v profiles against Ghia et al. — quantities where the literature already documents PINN errors on this exact benchmark. Without those, the null result is roughly "experts cannot distinguish two smoothed scalar fields," not "PINNs match CFD."

**The L2 baseline the paper frames itself against is never reported.** You motivate human eval as a *complement* to L2 error, then drop L2 entirely. Report it (versus a Ghia-style reference, ideally with relative errors on u, v, p, ω separately), so the reader can locate this PINN on the existing accuracy spectrum.

**The recommendation generalizes far beyond what was tested.** One steady 2D problem at Re = 1000, one quantity, one visualization → "default surrogate for incompressible-flow problems in the moderate-Reynolds regime" → "substitute in design optimization and UQ pipelines." Design optimization needs accurate sensitivities; UQ needs calibrated uncertainty; neither is probed. Lid-driven cavity at Re = 1000 is also the easiest canonical case — Re = 3200 / 5000 / 7500, backward-facing step, and any unsteady flow are where PINNs typically struggle. Scope the recommendation to the tested regime, or test more regimes.

**Smaller issues.** "Sampled uniformly along the trained PINN's solution trajectory" reads oddly for a steady benchmark — clarify whether these are spatial sub-windows, training-iteration snapshots, or a transient variant. "Now-standard composite-loss formulation" is editorial; just describe it. Per-expert range 44–61% deserves a brief note on whether top performers exceed chance after multiple-comparison correction. Confidence–accuracy r = 0.08 is consistent with experts having no signal *or* with confidence simply being miscalibrated; do not lean on it.

**Concrete revision path.** (1) Fix the two citation summaries — they are currently load-bearing and wrong. (2) Replace the affirmative conclusion with an equivalence-bounded one. (3) Add a second experiment on vorticity / divergence / centerline profiles, or substantially narrow the claim. (4) Report L2 / Ghia-reference errors alongside the human results. (5) Restrict the recommendation to "visually indistinguishable velocity-magnitude fields on steady 2D cavity flow at Re = 1000," and flag the open question for harder regimes.

Uncertainty: my reading of Krishnapriyan et al. (2021) and the Wang et al. PINN-weighting line is from memory and should be checked against the actual papers before you rewrite that paragraph; the rest of the critique stands independent of the citation details.
